originals: Many NLP applications require detecting relations between word meanings beyond synonymy and paraphrasing.
paraphrase_1: Beyond synonymy and paraphrasing, numerous NLP applications need to identify semantic relationships between words.
paraphrase_2: The detection of word meaning relationships that extend past synonymous and paraphrased connections is essential for various NLP applications.
paraphrase_3: Several natural language processing tasks demand the recognition of semantic associations among words that go further than simple synonym or paraphrase identification.
paraphrase_4: Identifying how word meanings relate to each other—in ways more complex than synonymy or paraphrasing—becomes crucial for multiple NLP systems.

originals: For instance, given “Carlsen plays chess.” and the question “Which game does Carlsen play?”, successfully answering the question requires knowing that chess is a kind of game, or more generally, that chess entails game.
paraphrase_1: To illustrate, when presented with "Carlsen plays chess" and asked "Which game does Carlsen play?", providing a correct response depends on understanding that chess constitutes a type of game, or broadly speaking, that the concept of chess implies the concept of game.
paraphrase_2: As an example, if someone states "Carlsen plays chess" and we ask "Which game does Carlsen play?", answering this question correctly requires recognizing that chess represents a form of game, or in general terms, that chess involves the broader category of game.
paraphrase_3: Consider this case: when "Carlsen plays chess" is given and the inquiry "Which game does Carlsen play?" follows, the ability to answer properly hinges on grasping that chess falls under the category of games, or more universally, that game is entailed by chess.
paraphrase_4: Take this scenario where we have "Carlsen plays chess" and someone poses the question "Which game does Carlsen play?" - responding accurately demands awareness that chess belongs to the class of games, or put differently, that chess necessarily involves the notion of a game.

originals: While prior work has defined lexical entailment as a relation between word types (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we argue entailment relations are better defined when illustrating word meaning with an example in context.
paraphrase_1: Although previous research has characterized lexical entailment as a relationship among word categories (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we contend that entailment relationships are more effectively established through contextual examples that demonstrate word meaning.
paraphrase_2: We propose that entailment relations should be defined by demonstrating word meanings through contextual examples, rather than treating them as connections between word types as previous studies have done (Turney and Mohammad, 2013).
paraphrase_3: In contrast to earlier scholarship that conceptualized lexical entailment as existing between word types (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), our position is that such relations achieve better definition when word meanings are illustrated using examples within context.
paraphrase_4: Past research by Turney and Mohammad (2013) approached lexical entailment as a word-type relationship, but we maintain that contextual examples showcasing word meaning provide a superior foundation for defining these entailment relations.

originals: Ignoring context is problematic since entailment might hold between some senses of the words, but not others.
paraphrase_1: The failure to consider context creates issues because entailment may exist for certain word meanings while being absent for others.
paraphrase_2: When context gets overlooked, problems arise as entailment could be valid for particular interpretations of words but invalid for different ones.
paraphrase_3: Context cannot be disregarded without causing difficulties, given that entailment relationships may apply to specific word senses yet fail to apply to alternative senses.
paraphrase_4: Problems emerge from neglecting context because while entailment might be present in some word interpretations, it could be completely lacking in others.

originals: In this paper, we investigate how to represent and compare the meaning of words in context for lexical entailment.
paraphrase_1: This study explores methods for encoding and contrasting contextual word meanings to determine lexical entailment relationships.
paraphrase_2: The research examines approaches to modeling and evaluating semantic representations of words within their contexts for the purpose of lexical entailment analysis.
paraphrase_3: Our work focuses on developing techniques that capture and assess how word meanings function in specific contexts when establishing lexical entailment.
paraphrase_4: We present an investigation into the representation and comparison of context-dependent word semantics as they relate to lexical entailment phenomena.

originals: Since distributional representations for word types have proved useful to detect lexical entailment out of context in supervised settings (Baroni et al., 2012; Roller et al., 2014; Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we propose to transform context-agnostic word type representations into contextualized representations that highlight salient properties of the context (Section 3), and use these contextualized representations with a range of semantic similarity features (Section 4) to successfully detect entailment.
paraphrase_1: Given that word type distributional representations have demonstrated effectiveness in identifying lexical entailment without contextual information in supervised frameworks (Baroni et al., 2012; Roller et al., 2014; Turney and Mohammad, 2013), our approach involves converting these context-independent word type representations into context-sensitive versions that emphasize important contextual characteristics (Section 3), then applying these context-aware representations alongside various semantic similarity features (Section 4) to achieve successful entailment detection.
paraphrase_2: We suggest converting context-independent word type representations into contextually-aware versions that accentuate relevant contextual features (Section 3) and employing these alongside diverse semantic similarity measures (Section 4) for effective entailment recognition, building on evidence that distributional word type representations have proven valuable for supervised lexical entailment detection in context-free scenarios (Baroni et al., 2012; Roller et al., 2014; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).
paraphrase_3: Because research has shown distributional word type representations to be effective for context-free lexical entailment detection under supervised conditions (Baroni et al., 2012; Roller et al., 2014; Turney and Mohammad, 2013), our methodology transforms these context-agnostic representations into contextualized ones that foreground significant contextual attributes (Section 3), utilizing them with multiple semantic similarity features (Section 4) to accomplish entailment detection successfully.
paraphrase_4: The proven utility of distributional word type representations in supervised lexical entailment detection without contextual considerations (Baroni et al., 2012; Roller et al., 2014; Turney and Mohammad, 2013) motivates our proposal to modify context-neutral word type representations into context-specific versions that spotlight pertinent contextual elements (Section 3), combining these with varied semantic similarity features (Section 4) to effectively identify entailment.

originals: As we will see, these context representations significantly improve performance over contextagnostic baselines not only in English, but also between English and French words (Section 7) on two novel datasets (Section 5).
paraphrase_1: Our findings demonstrate that context representations deliver substantial performance gains compared to context-agnostic baselines across both English and cross-lingual English-French word pairs, as evaluated on two new datasets we introduce.
paraphrase_2: The results reveal significant enhancements in performance when using context representations versus baselines that ignore context, with improvements observed not just for English but also for English-French word relationships across two newly created datasets.
paraphrase_3: Context-aware representations outperform their context-blind counterparts by considerable margins, showing improvements in both monolingual English tasks and bilingual English-French word analysis using our two original datasets.
paraphrase_4: Performance is substantially enhanced through context representations relative to context-independent methods, with notable improvements spanning English tasks and English-French cross-linguistic scenarios across our two novel experimental datasets.

originals: We also show that our features are sensitive to word sense changes indicated by context, and adequately capture the direction of entailment relation (Section 8).
paraphrase_1: Additionally, we demonstrate that our features respond to contextual shifts in word meaning and effectively represent the directional nature of entailment relationships (Section 8).
paraphrase_2: Section 8 reveals that contextual variations in word sense are detected by our features, which also successfully encode entailment directionality.
paraphrase_3: Our findings indicate that the proposed features not only react to context-driven semantic changes in words but also properly model how entailment flows in one direction (see Section 8).
paraphrase_4: The sensitivity of our features to context-dependent word sense disambiguation is established, along with their ability to appropriately represent entailment's unidirectional character, as discussed in Section 8.

originals: Moreover, we establish a new state-of-the-art on an existing dataset that captures a broader range of semantic relations in context (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015), and show that the proposed features, induced solely from large amounts of raw text, yield systems that perform as well, or better than existing systems that require additional human annotation.
paraphrase_1: Additionally, our approach achieves record-breaking performance on a previously established dataset encompassing diverse contextual semantic relationships (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015), while demonstrating that features derived exclusively from extensive raw text corpora produce systems matching or surpassing existing methods that depend on supplementary human-annotated data.
paraphrase_2: We also set a new performance benchmark on an established dataset designed to encompass wider semantic relationship varieties within context (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015), proving that systems built using our text-only derived features can equal or outperform current approaches requiring extra manual annotations.
paraphrase_3: Furthermore, record-setting results are achieved on an existing dataset that represents broader contextual semantic relations (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015), with our purely text-based feature extraction enabling system performance that rivals or exceeds methods dependent on additional human labeling efforts.
paraphrase_4: Our work also delivers unprecedented results on a dataset previously created to capture extensive semantic relationship types in context (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015), establishing that features extracted from raw text alone can drive systems to performance levels comparable to or superior to those requiring human annotation supplements.

Words (wl, wr) Exemplars (cl,cr) Does wl =⇒ wr ?
paraphrase_1: The lexical entailment task in context is structured as binary classification using 4-tuple examples (wl, wr, cl, cr), with wl and wr representing two words and cl and cr representing sentences that contain these respective words.
paraphrase_2: Binary classification is applied to the contextual lexical entailment problem, where each example takes the form of a 4-tuple (wl, wr, cl, cr) - here, wl and wr denote word pairs while cl and cr denote the sentences in which they appear.
paraphrase_3: As a binary classification problem, we approach contextual lexical entailment by examining 4-tuples (wl, wr, cl, cr), in which two words (wl, wr) are paired with their corresponding contextual sentences (cl, cr).
paraphrase_4: Our approach treats lexical entailment within context as binary classification, processing examples that consist of 4-tuples containing word pairs wl and wr alongside their respective sentential contexts cl and cr.

originals: Recognizing textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2013) and natural language inference (Bowman et al., 2015) involves detecting entailment relations between sentences, while lexical entailment is a relation between words.

paraphrase_2: The tasks of recognizing textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2013) and natural language inference (Bowman et al., 2015) require identifying when one sentence entails another, whereas lexical entailment operates on word-to-word relationships.

paraphrase_4: Detection of entailment relations between sentences is the goal of textual entailment recognition (Dagan et al., 2013) and natural language inference (Bowman et al., 2015), distinguishing these tasks from lexical entailment that deals with word-level relationships.

originals: An alternative approach to contextualizing word representations is to directly compare the representations of words with representations of contexts.
paraphrase_1: A different method for providing context to word representations involves making direct comparisons between word representations and the representations of their surrounding contexts.
paraphrase_2: Rather than using traditional contextualization techniques, one can establish contextual meaning by contrasting how words are represented against how their contextual environments are represented.
paraphrase_3: Word representations can be contextualized through an alternative strategy that directly contrasts representations of individual words with those of their corresponding contexts.
paraphrase_4: Instead of conventional approaches, contextual understanding of word representations may be achieved by performing direct comparisons between the way words and their contexts are each represented.

originals: In a single language, this can be done using Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016), a neural model that, given a target word and its sentential context, embeds both the word and the context in the same low-dimensional space, with the objective of having the context predict the target word via a log linear model.
paraphrase_1: Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016) offers a neural approach for monolingual tasks by placing target words and their surrounding sentence contexts into a shared low-dimensional embedding space, where the context serves to predict the target word through a log linear framework.
paraphrase_2: For monolingual applications, researchers can employ Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016), which represents both target words and their sentential environments as vectors in the same reduced-dimension space, enabling context-based word prediction via log linear modeling.
paraphrase_3: Within a single language, the neural architecture Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016) accomplishes this by creating unified low-dimensional representations of words and their sentence-level contexts, designed so that contextual information can effectively predict target words using log linear techniques.
paraphrase_4: The Context2Vec model by Melamud et al. (2016) provides a solution for single-language scenarios through its neural network that maps target words alongside their contextual sentences into a common vector space of reduced dimensions, where log linear prediction links contexts to their corresponding target words.

originals: This model approaches the state-of-the-art on lexical substitution, sentence completion, and supervised word sense disambiguation.

paraphrase_2: State-of-the-art results are nearly matched by this model across three areas: substituting words lexically, completing sentences, and disambiguating word meanings under supervision.
paraphrase_3: The performance of this model comes close to cutting-edge standards in supervised word sense disambiguation, lexical substitution, and sentence completion.
paraphrase_4: In the domains of sentence completion, lexical word replacement, and supervised disambiguation of word meanings, this model delivers near-optimal performance.

originals: Prior work on lexical entailment out of context suggests that the entailment relationship between words is a learnable function of the concatenation of their individual representations (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).
paraphrase_1: Research conducted previously on lexical entailment without contextual information indicates that word-level entailment relations can be learned as a function operating on combined individual word representations (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).
paraphrase_2: Earlier studies examining lexical entailment in isolation from context demonstrate that entailment connections between words represent a function that can be acquired through the concatenation of their separate vector representations (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).
paraphrase_3: According to previous research on context-independent lexical entailment, the entailment relations that exist between words can be modeled as a learnable function based on joining their distinct representational vectors (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).
paraphrase_4: Past investigations into lexical entailment performed outside of contextual frameworks have shown that entailment patterns between words constitute a function that machines can learn from concatenated individual word embeddings (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).

originals: After concatenation, they are used as features for a logistic regression (Roller et al., 2014) or an SVM classifier (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).

paraphrase_4: Researchers employ the merged vectors as classification features, utilizing them in support vector machines (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013) or logistic regression frameworks (Roller et al., 2014).

originals: We hypothesize that entailment relations hold between related words and introduce similarity features to capture this non-directional relation between words and contexts.
paraphrase_1: Our hypothesis proposes that related words exhibit entailment relationships, leading us to develop similarity features that model this bidirectional connection between words and their contexts.
paraphrase_2: The assumption underlying our approach is that entailment connections exist among semantically related terms, prompting the incorporation of similarity measures to represent these undirected word-context associations.
paraphrase_3: By positing that semantically connected words maintain entailment links, we present similarity-based features designed to encode the non-directional nature of word-context relationships.
paraphrase_4: Entailment relationships are theorized to exist between words that share semantic connections, which motivates our introduction of similarity features for capturing these symmetric word-context interactions.

originals: Modeling word meaning in context Several approaches have been proposed to model the meaning of a word in a given context to capture semantic equivalence in tasks such as lexical substitution, word sense disambiguation or paraphrase ranking (but not entailment).
paraphrase_1: Various methods have been developed for representing contextual word semantics, enabling the identification of semantic similarity in applications like word replacement, sense identification, and paraphrase evaluation (excluding entailment tasks).
paraphrase_2: To capture semantic equivalence for lexical substitution, word sense disambiguation, and paraphrase ranking tasks (though not entailment), researchers have put forward multiple techniques for contextual word meaning representation.
paraphrase_3: The challenge of contextually modeling word semantics has led to numerous proposed solutions, designed to detect semantic similarity across tasks including paraphrase evaluation, sense disambiguation, and lexical replacement (but not including entailment).
paraphrase_4: Different strategies for capturing how words convey meaning within specific contexts have been introduced, targeting semantic equivalence detection in areas such as substitution of lexical items, disambiguation of word senses, and ranking of paraphrases (with entailment being excluded).

originals: One line of work (Dinu and Lapata, 2010; Reisinger and Mooney, 2010) treats each word as a set of latent word senses.
paraphrase_1: A particular research approach (Dinu and Lapata, 2010; Reisinger and Mooney, 2010) conceptualizes individual words as collections of hidden semantic meanings.
paraphrase_2: Certain studies (Dinu and Lapata, 2010; Reisinger and Mooney, 2010) model words by representing them as groups of underlying sense interpretations.

originals: Apidianaki (2016) shows that bag-ofword context representation within a small context window works as well as syntactic definitions of context for ranking paraphrases in context.
paraphrase_1: The research conducted by Apidianaki (2016) demonstrates that utilizing bag-of-word context representations from narrow contextual windows performs comparably to syntax-based contextual definitions when it comes to contextually ranking paraphrases.
paraphrase_2: According to Apidianaki (2016), paraphrase ranking in context achieves equivalent effectiveness whether employing bag-of-word representations from limited surrounding text or relying on syntactically-defined contextual approaches.
paraphrase_3: Apidianaki's 2016 findings reveal that for the task of ranking paraphrases within their contexts, small-window bag-of-word contextual representations match the performance of approaches that define context through syntactic structures.
paraphrase_4: In 2016, Apidianaki established that when ranking paraphrases contextually, bag-of-word context modeling using restricted context windows delivers performance levels equivalent to those achieved by syntactic context definition methods.

originals: In-context lexical semantic tasks Besides entailment, other lexical semantic tasks studied in the presence of context include lexical substitution (McCarthy and Navigli, 2007), cross-lingual lexical substitution (Mihalcea et al., 2010) and paraphrase ranking (Apidianaki, 2016).
paraphrase_1: Beyond entailment, researchers have examined additional lexical semantic tasks within contextual frameworks, such as lexical substitution (McCarthy and Navigli, 2007), cross-lingual lexical substitution (Mihalcea et al., 2010), and paraphrase ranking (Apidianaki, 2016).
paraphrase_2: Other contextual lexical semantic tasks that have been investigated apart from entailment are lexical substitution (McCarthy and Navigli, 2007), paraphrase ranking (Apidianaki, 2016), and cross-lingual lexical substitution (Mihalcea et al., 2010).
paraphrase_3: Lexical substitution (McCarthy and Navigli, 2007), cross-lingual lexical substitution (Mihalcea et al., 2010), and paraphrase ranking (Apidianaki, 2016) represent additional lexical semantic tasks that have been explored in contextual settings, alongside entailment.
paraphrase_4: Contextual lexical semantic research encompasses not only entailment but also tasks like lexical substitution (McCarthy and Navigli, 2007), its cross-lingual variant (Mihalcea et al., 2010), and paraphrase ranking (Apidianaki, 2016).

originals: The last work is also notable because of their successful use of models of word-meaning in context from Thater et al (2011), which is closely related to our methods.

paraphrase_2: What makes the concluding research particularly significant is how it successfully employed the contextual word-meaning frameworks proposed by Thater et al (2011), which bear close resemblance to the techniques we utilize.

paraphrase_4: The final work merits recognition because it effectively leveraged contextual word-meaning modeling techniques from Thater et al (2011), methodologies that demonstrate significant similarity to our research approaches.

originals: We proposed to address lexical entailment in context, providing exemplar sentences to ground the meaning of words being considered for entailment.
paraphrase_1: Our approach tackled contextual lexical entailment by supplying example sentences that establish the meanings of words under consideration for entailment relationships.
paraphrase_2: To handle lexical entailment within specific contexts, we suggested using illustrative sentences that anchor the semantic interpretations of candidate entailment terms.
paraphrase_3: The method we put forward involves contextualizing lexical entailment through the provision of sample sentences that clarify how words should be understood in entailment scenarios.
paraphrase_4: By offering concrete sentence examples that define the relevant word meanings, our proposal sought to resolve lexical entailment problems in their proper contexts.

originals: We show that contextualized word representations constructed by transforming contextagnostic representations, combined with wordcontext similarity features, lead to large improvements over context-agnostic model, not only in English, but also between English and French words on two novel datasets.
paraphrase_1: Our findings demonstrate that when context-agnostic representations are transformed into contextualized word representations and paired with word-context similarity features, they produce significant enhancements compared to context-agnostic models across English and in cross-lingual English-French scenarios using two new datasets.
paraphrase_2: By transforming context-agnostic representations into contextualized word representations and incorporating word-context similarity features, substantial performance gains are achieved over context-agnostic approaches, as evidenced in both monolingual English tasks and bilingual English-French evaluations on two newly introduced datasets.
paraphrase_3: The research reveals that contextualized word representations, derived through transformation of context-agnostic representations and enhanced with word-context similarity features, significantly outperform context-agnostic models not just for English but also for English-French language pairs across two original datasets.
paraphrase_4: Large-scale improvements over context-agnostic models are observed when context-agnostic representations undergo transformation to create contextualized word representations, which are then augmented with word-context similarity features—a pattern consistent across English monolingual tasks and English-French bilingual evaluations on two newly developed datasets.

originals: We also improve the state-of-the-art on a related task of detecting semantic relations in context.
paraphrase_1: Additionally, we achieve superior performance compared to existing methods on the contextual semantic relation detection task.
paraphrase_2: Our approach also establishes new benchmarks for identifying semantic relationships within contextual frameworks.
paraphrase_3: Furthermore, enhanced results are delivered by our method on the associated challenge of recognizing semantic connections in situational contexts.
paraphrase_4: The detection of semantic relations in context—a closely related problem—also sees advancement through our contributions to the current best results.

originals: Our features are sensitive to changes in entailment based on context, and also capture the directionality of entailment.
paraphrase_1: The features we developed respond to contextual shifts in entailment relationships while simultaneously encoding whether entailment flows in one direction or another.
paraphrase_2: Both the sensitivity to context-dependent entailment variations and the ability to detect entailment direction are captured by our feature set.
paraphrase_3: Changes in entailment that depend on context trigger responses in our features, which additionally encode the directional nature of entailment relationships.
paraphrase_4: Our feature design ensures responsiveness to how context alters entailment while also representing the asymmetric properties of entailment connections.

originals: In future work, we aim to further improve performance on both monolingual and cross-lingual datasets.
paraphrase_1: Our future research will focus on enhancing results across datasets in single languages as well as those spanning multiple languages.
paraphrase_2: Moving forward, we plan to achieve better outcomes for both within-language and across-language dataset evaluations.
paraphrase_3: Subsequent studies will target performance improvements on datasets involving one language and those incorporating several languages.
paraphrase_4: We intend to advance our methodology's effectiveness on both single-language and multilingual dataset benchmarks in upcoming research.

originals: We anticipate that richer features, capturing second-order comparisons used to detect lexical contrast (Mohammad et al., 2013) and entailment out of context (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), might be useful for this purpose, perhaps in combination with non-linear classifiers.
paraphrase_1: For this purpose, we expect that more sophisticated features—those that capture second-order comparisons for identifying lexical contrast (Mohammad et al., 2013) and context-independent entailment (Turney and Mohammad, 2013)—could prove beneficial, possibly when paired with non-linear classification methods.
paraphrase_2: It is likely that enhanced feature sets would serve this objective well, particularly those designed to extract second-order comparative information for lexical contrast detection (Mohammad et al., 2013) and out-of-context entailment recognition (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), especially when combined with non-linear classification approaches.
paraphrase_3: Second-order comparison features that enable lexical contrast detection (Mohammad et al., 2013) and context-free entailment identification (Turney and Mohammad, 2013) may offer value for this task, and their utility could be amplified through integration with non-linear classifier architectures.
paraphrase_4: The incorporation of more complex features—specifically those leveraging second-order comparisons to identify lexical contrasts (Mohammad et al., 2013) and entailments independent of context (Turney and Mohammad, 2013)—holds promise for this application, particularly alongside non-linear classification techniques.
